explanation: This appears to be a label artifact combining 'Sodium Hyaluronate' (the INCI salt form) with 'Acid' (from 'Hyaluronic Acid') — it refers to sodium hyaluronate, a powerful humectant that forms a water-binding film on the skin for immediate hydration and a plumping effect. It is exceptionally well-tolerated by all skin types and among the most reliably safe and effective humectants in modern skincare.

Sodium hyaluronate crosspolymer is a crosslinked form of sodium hyaluronate that forms a longer-lasting, more substantive moisture-retaining film on the skin compared to linear sodium hyaluronate, providing extended hydration and a plumping effect with improved wear. It is exceptionally well-tolerated and used in moisturizing formulas for its superior durable hydration profile.

Sodium hyaluronate dimethylsilanol is a silicone-modified sodium hyaluronate combining the humectant hydration of hyaluronic acid with the film-forming and smoothing properties of silicone, providing both moisture retention and a silky skin feel. It is well-tolerated and used in premium moisturizing formulas for its dual silicone-hyaluronate benefit.

Sodium hydrolyzed potato starch dodecenylsuccinate is a modified potato starch esterified with dodecenylsuccinic acid anhydride, used as a mild emulsifier, thickener, and texture modifier derived from natural starch. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations, functioning as a natural-derived texture and emulsion stabilizer.

Sodium hydroxide (lye, NaOH) is a strong base used in skincare formulas exclusively as a pH adjuster to neutralize acids and bring the formula to its intended pH — it is fully neutralized in the finished product and does not contact skin as a free caustic. It is considered non-irritating in properly buffered finished products.

Sodium isethionate is the sodium salt of isethionic acid used as a mild skin-conditioning agent and co-ingredient that contributes to the mildness and skin compatibility of isethionate-based cleansing systems. It is considered well-tolerated at typical cosmetic use concentrations, functioning as a conditioning aid in gentle cleanser formulations.

Sodium lactate is the sodium salt of lactic acid — a natural component of the skin's NMF (natural moisturizing factor) — used as a potent humectant and pH buffer that draws moisture to the skin while maintaining formula stability. It is well-tolerated and used in moisturizing and hydrating formulas for its biomimetic NMF-derived humectant properties.

Sodium laureth sulfate (SLES) is an ethoxylated version of SLS — the most widely used anionic surfactant in cleansers — which is milder than SLS due to ethoxylation but still a primary irritant capable of stripping the skin's lipid barrier at higher concentrations. It is flagged as a sulfate surfactant; individuals with compromised barriers, eczema, or reactive skin should prefer non-sulfate alternatives.

Sodium lauroyl lactylate is an ester of lactic acid and lauric acid used as a mild emulsifier and conditioning surfactant with both cleansing and skin-conditioning properties, compatible with a wide range of formula pH levels. It is well-tolerated and used in gentle cleansing and conditioning formulas for its mild, skin-compatible emulsification profile.

Sodium lauroyl methyl isethionate is a mild isethionate-based surfactant derived from lauric acid (coconut oil), providing effective cleansing with a gentle, skin-compatible profile similar to sodium cocoyl isethionate. It is well-tolerated and used in gentle cleansers and syndet bars for its mildness and skin-comfortable cleansing.

Sodium lauroyl sarcosinate is a mild amino acid-derived anionic surfactant (sarcosine derivative) that provides gentle cleansing with significantly lower irritation potential than SLS, along with mild conditioning properties. It is well-tolerated and used in gentle cleansers and shampoos for its mild biodegradable sarcosinate surfactant profile.

Sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS) is the most widely studied anionic sulfate surfactant — the benchmark irritant used in controlled sensitivity studies — that provides powerful cleansing but is a well-documented primary skin irritant capable of stripping the lipid barrier and increasing transepidermal water loss. It is flagged as a sulfate surfactant; sensitive, reactive, eczema-prone, and barrier-compromised skin should avoid it, particularly in leave-on or prolonged-contact products.

Sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS) — a duplicate entry — is the benchmark anionic sulfate surfactant that is a well-documented primary skin irritant capable of stripping the lipid barrier and triggering inflammation at effective cleansing concentrations. It is flagged as a sulfate surfactant; sensitive and reactive skin should prefer non-sulfate cleansing alternatives.

Sodium laurylglucosides hydroxypropylsulfonate is a hybrid surfactant combining the gentle alkyl glucoside structure with a hydroxypropylsulfonate group, providing effective cleansing with a more skin-compatible profile than standard sulfate surfactants. It is considered well-tolerated at typical rinse-off concentrations and used in mild cleansers for its improved mildness.

Sodium metabisulfite is an inorganic sulfite salt used as an antioxidant preservative that scavenges dissolved oxygen in aqueous formulas to prevent oxidative degradation of active ingredients. It is generally well-tolerated at cosmetic concentrations; individuals with sulfite sensitivity — particularly those with sulfite-sensitive asthma — should note its sulfite composition.

Sodium methyl cocoyl taurate is a mild taurate-based surfactant derived from coconut fatty acids and the amino acid-like taurine, providing gentle, effective cleansing with excellent skin compatibility. It is well-tolerated and used in premium gentle cleansers for its mild, biodegradable taurate surfactant profile.

Sodium myreth sulfate is an ethoxylated myristyl alcohol sulfate surfactant used in shampoos and cleansers — similar to SLES but from myristyl (C14) rather than lauryl (C12) alcohol — providing effective cleansing with a profile similar to SLES. It is flagged as a sulfate surfactant; individuals with sensitive or reactive skin should prefer non-sulfate alternatives.

Sodium palmitoyl proline is a fatty acid-amino acid conjugate combining palmitic acid (C16) with the collagen amino acid proline, delivering a proline-rich collagen-precursor signal in a lipophilic form that supports skin firmness. It is well-tolerated and used in firming and anti-aging formulas for its collagen-precursor amino acid delivery.

Sodium PCA is the sodium salt of pyroglutamic acid (PCA) — one of the most critical components of the skin's natural moisturizing factor (NMF) — that acts as a potent humectant naturally present in the stratum corneum, drawing ambient moisture into the skin with exceptional efficiency. It is exceptionally well-tolerated and biomimetic, providing particularly effective hydration for dry and dehydrated skin by replenishing NMF components.

Sodium phytate is the sodium salt of phytic acid (inositol hexaphosphate) used in skincare as a chelating agent and antioxidant that binds metal ions (iron, copper) which would otherwise catalyze oxidative degradation of active formula ingredients. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing, functioning as a formula stabilizer and preservative efficacy booster.

Sodium polyacrylate is a crosslinked synthetic acrylate polymer used as a highly efficient thickener, superabsorbent agent, and texture modifier in water-based skincare formulas, providing a smooth, lightweight gel texture with excellent suspension properties. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Sodium polyacryloyldimethyl taurate is a taurate-modified crosslinked acrylate polymer used as a thickener and rheology modifier in water-based skincare formulas, providing a smooth, elegant gel texture with good suspension and emulsion stability. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing at typical cosmetic use concentrations.

Sodium shea butteramphoacetate is an amphoteric acetate surfactant derived from shea butter fatty acids, used as a mild co-surfactant in cleansers that provides conditioning and gentle cleansing with shea-derived fatty acid benefits. It is well-tolerated, non-sensitizing, and used in gentle cleansers for its combined mild cleansing and conditioning properties.

Sodium shea butterate is the sodium salt of shea butter fatty acids produced by saponification, used as a mild soap-based cleanser and conditioning ingredient with the skin-compatible fatty acid profile of shea butter. It is well-tolerated and used in natural cleansing formulas for its gentle shea-derived surfactant properties.

Sodium stearate is the sodium salt of stearic acid — the traditional soap molecule formed from saponification of stearic acid — used in cosmetics as a cleanser, emulsifier, and structure-forming ingredient in bar soaps and solid cosmetics. It is generally well-tolerated; its alkaline pH in water (soap ~pH 9–10) can disrupt the skin's acid mantle with frequent use on sensitive or compromised skin.

Sodium sulfate (Na₂SO₄) is an inorganic salt used in cosmetics and cleansing formulas primarily as a viscosity modifier for surfactant systems and as a filler in powder formulas. It is considered inert, non-sensitizing, and well-tolerated at typical cosmetic use concentrations, functioning as a formulation aid rather than a skin-active ingredient.

Sodium surfactin is a biosurfactant cyclic lipopeptide produced by Bacillus subtilis bacteria, providing exceptionally mild, biodegradable surfactant activity with additional antimicrobial properties. It is well-tolerated, eco-friendly, and used in green-chemistry and natural cleansing formulas as a gentle, sustainable alternative to synthetic surfactants.

Solanum melongena (eggplant/aubergine) fruit extract provides delphinidin anthocyanins, chlorogenic acid, and polyphenols with free-radical-scavenging and brightening properties. It is well-tolerated and used in antioxidant and protective formulas for its polyphenol and anthocyanin content.

Sophora japonica (Japanese pagoda tree) fruit extract is one of the richest natural sources of the bioflavonoid rutin, along with quercetin and kaempferol, with potent capillary-strengthening, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. It is well-tolerated and used in anti-redness, brightening, and protective formulas for its exceptional rutin and quercetin antioxidant content.

Sorbeth-30 tetraoleate is a high-ethoxylation sorbitan tetraoleate ester used as a mild emulsifier and solubilizer in skincare formulas, with the high ethoxylation number contributing to greater mildness. It is generally well-tolerated at typical cosmetic use concentrations; individuals with PEG sensitivity should note the ethoxylate modification.
